***Banksia serrata***, commonly known as the **saw banksia**,[[2]](#cite_note-RBGS-2) the **old man banksia**,[[3]](#cite_note-NP&WS-3) the **saw-tooth banksia** or the **red honeysuckle** and as **wiriyagan** by the [Cadigal](/wiki/Cadigal "Cadigal") people, is a species of woody [shrub](/wiki/Shrub "Shrub") or tree of the genus *[Banksia](/wiki/Banksia "Banksia")*, in the family [Proteaceae](/wiki/Proteaceae "Proteaceae"). [Native](/wiki/Indigenous_(ecology) "Indigenous (ecology)") to the east coast of Australia, it is found from [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") to [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Australia "Victoria, Australia") with outlying populations on [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania") and [Flinders Island](/wiki/Flinders_Island "Flinders Island"). Commonly growing as a gnarled tree up to 16 m (50 ft) in height, it can be much smaller in more exposed areas. This *Banksia* species has wrinkled grey bark, shiny dark green serrated leaves and large yellow or greyish-yellow flower spikes appearing over summer. The flower spikes, or [inflorescences](/wiki/Inflorescence "Inflorescence"), turn grey as they age and pollinated flowers develop into large, grey, woody seed pods called [follicles](/wiki/Follicle_(fruit) "Follicle (fruit)").

*B. serrata* is one of the four original *Banksia* species collected by [Sir Joseph Banks](/wiki/Sir_Joseph_Banks "Sir Joseph Banks") in 1770, and one of four species published in 1782 as part of [Carolus Linnaeus the Younger](/wiki/Carolus_Linnaeus_the_Younger "Carolus Linnaeus the Younger")'s original [description](/wiki/Species_description "Species description") of the genus. There are no recognised [varieties](/wiki/Variety_(botany) "Variety (botany)"), although it is closely related to *[Banksia aemula](/wiki/Banksia_aemula "Banksia aemula")*. Throughout its range, it grows exclusively in sandy soil, and is usually the dominant plant in [scrubland](/wiki/Scrubland "Scrubland") or low [woodland](/wiki/Woodland "Woodland"). *B. serrata* is [pollinated](/wiki/Pollination "Pollination") by and provides food for a wide array of [vertebrate](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate") and [invertebrate](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate") animals in the autumn and winter months, and is an important source of food for [honeyeaters](/wiki/Honeyeater "Honeyeater"). It is a common plant of parks and gardens.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Banksia_serrata&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

*Banksia serrata* usually grows as a gnarled and misshapen tree up to 16 m (50 ft) tall, although in some coastal habitats it grows as a shrub of 1–3 m (3–10 ft), and on exposed coastal cliffs it has even been recorded as a [prostrate shrub](/wiki/Prostrate_shrub "Prostrate shrub"). As a tree it usually has a single, stout trunk with warty, [knobbly](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/knobbly "wikt:knobbly") grey bark up to 3 cm (1.2 in) thick. Trunks are often black from past [bushfires](/wiki/Bushfires_in_Australia "Bushfires in Australia"), and ooze a red sap when injured. New growth appears in spring, summer and autumn. New [branchlets](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/branchlet "wikt:branchlet") are hairy, remaining so for two to three years. Leaves are usually crowded together at the upper end of branches, giving the [canopy](/wiki/Canopy_(biology) "Canopy (biology)") a thin, sparse appearance. The leaves themselves are dark glossy green above and light green below, 7 to 20 cm (2.8 to 7.9 in)—rarely up to 26 cm (10 in)—long by 2 to 4 cm (0.8 to 1.6 in)—rarely up to 4.5 cm (1.8 in)—wide, and oblong to [obovate](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/obovate "wikt:obovate") (egg-shaped) in shape. The leaf margins are serrated, except near the base, with lobes between 1 and 3 mm (0.04 and 0.12 in) deep.[[4]](#cite_note-George_1981-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Taylor_1988-5)

Cylindrical flower spikes, or [inflorescences](/wiki/Inflorescence "Inflorescence"), grow from the ends of 1- to 2-year-old branchlets and have leaves at their base. The spikes are generally 9 to 12 cm (3.5 to 4.7 in) wide with hundreds of individual flowers arising from an upright woody axis. The woody axis is 7 to 15 cm (2.8 to 5.9 in) high and 0.9 to 1 cm (0.35 to 0.39 in) wide. The flowers are cream-grey in colour with cream [styles](/wiki/Gynoecium#Pistils "Gynoecium"). Old flower spikes develop into "cones" that consist of up to thirty [follicles](/wiki/Follicle_(fruit) "Follicle (fruit)") that develop from the flowers that were pollinated. Old withered flower parts remain on the cones, giving them a hairy appearance. Each follicle is oval in shape, wrinkled in texture, covered with fine hair and 2.5–3.5 cm (1.0–1.4 in) long, 2.0–2.5 cm (0.8–1.0 in) thick, and 1.5–2.2 cm (0.6–0.9 in) wide.[[4]](#cite_note-George_1981-4)

The obovate seed is 3–3.4 cm (1.2–1.3 in) long, fairly flattened, has a papery [wing](/wiki/Glossary_of_botanical_terms#W "Glossary of botanical terms") and weighs around 77.5 mg (0.0027 oz). The seed is composed of the obovate seed body (containing the [embryonic](/wiki/Plant_embryogenesis "Plant embryogenesis") plant), which measures 1.0–1.2 cm (0.39–0.47 in) long by 0.9–1.1 cm (0.35–0.43 in) wide. One side, termed the outer surface, is pitted and dark brown and the other is brown-black and warty, sparkling slightly. The seeds are separated in the follicle by a sturdy dark brown [seed separator](/wiki/Seed_separator "Seed separator"), which is about the same shape as the seeds, with a depression where the seed body sits adjacent to it. The first pair of leaves (called *[cotyledons](/wiki/Cotyledon "Cotyledon")*) produced by seedlings are obovate, dull green and measure 1–1.4 cm (0.39–0.55 in) long by 1–1.5 cm (0.4–0.6 in) wide. The [auricle](/wiki/Auricle_(botany) "Auricle (botany)") at the base of the cotyledon leaf is pointed and measures 0.2 cm (0.08 in) long. The [hypocotyl](/wiki/Hypocotyl "Hypocotyl") is thick, hairy and red. The cotyledons are linear to lance-shaped with the narrow end towards the base, 3.5–10 cm (1.4–3.9 in) long with serrated margins and a v-shaped [sinus](/wiki/Sinus_(botany) "Sinus (botany)") at the tip.[[4]](#cite_note-George_1981-4) *Banksia serrata* closely resembles *[B. aemula](/wiki/Banksia_aemula "Banksia aemula")*, but the latter can be distinguished by an orange-brown, rather than greyish, trunk, and adult leaves narrower than 2 cm (0.8 in). The inflorescences of *B. serrata* are generally a duller grey-yellow in colour, have longer (2–3 mm), more [fusiform](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/fusiform "wikt:fusiform") or cylindrical pollen presenters on the tips of unopened flowers and the follicles are smaller.[[6]](#cite_note-Walters-6)[[7]](#cite_note-PoNSW-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Wrigley_1991-8)

## Taxonomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Banksia_serrata&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Taxonomy")]

*Banksia serrata* was first collected at [Botany Bay](/wiki/Botany_Bay "Botany Bay") on 29 April 1770, by [Sir Joseph Banks](/wiki/Joseph_Banks "Joseph Banks") and [Daniel Solander](/wiki/Daniel_Solander "Daniel Solander"), naturalists on the British vessel *[HMS Endeavour](/wiki/HMS_Endeavour "HMS Endeavour")* during Lieutenant (later Captain) [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook")'s [first voyage](/wiki/First_voyage_of_James_Cook "First voyage of James Cook") to the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean").[[9]](#cite_note-Wharton_1893-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Banks_1770-10) Solander coined the (unpublished) binomial name *Leucadendrum serratifolium*, with *Leucadendron serratum* also appearing under the finished drawing in *[Banks' Florilegium](/wiki/Banks%27_Florilegium "Banks' Florilegium")*.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The first formal [description](/wiki/Species_description "Species description") of the species was not published until April 1782, when [Carolus Linnaeus the Younger](/wiki/Carolus_Linnaeus_the_Younger "Carolus Linnaeus the Younger") described the first four *Banksia* species in his *[Supplementum Plantarum](/wiki/Supplementum_Plantarum "Supplementum Plantarum")*, commenting that it was the showiest species in the genus.[[12]](#cite_note-L.f._1782-12) As the first named species of the genus, *Banksia serrata* is considered the [type species](/wiki/Type_species "Type species").[[4]](#cite_note-George_1981-4) *Banksia serrata* has the common names of old man banksia, saw banksia, saw-toothed banksia and saw-leaved banksia. It is also known as red honeysuckle and red banksia from the colour of its timber.[[8]](#cite_note-Wrigley_1991-8) The Cadigal people who lived in the Sydney region prior to the arrival of Europeans, called the plant wiriyagan.[[2]](#cite_note-RBGS-2)

German botanist [Joseph Gaertner](/wiki/Joseph_Gaertner "Joseph Gaertner") described *Banksia conchifera* in 1788 in the first volume of his work *[De Fructibus et Seminibus Plantarum](/wiki/De_Fructibus_et_Seminibus_Plantarum "De Fructibus et Seminibus Plantarum")*.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Alex George noted this description was taken from Linnaeus' original and was hence a *[nomen illegitimum](/wiki/Nomen_illegitimum "Nomen illegitimum")* (illegitimate name).[[4]](#cite_note-George_1981-4) [Joseph Knight](/wiki/Joseph_Knight_(horticulturist) "Joseph Knight (horticulturist)") described *Banksia mitis* and *Banksia serræfolia* in his 1809 *[On the cultivation of the plants belonging to the natural order of Proteeae](/wiki/On_the_cultivation_of_the_plants_belonging_to_the_natural_order_of_Proteeae "On the cultivation of the plants belonging to the natural order of Proteeae")*, both later determined to be *B. serrata* and hence superfluous. In 1830, [John Lindley](/wiki/John_Lindley "John Lindley") wrote of a plant in cultivation in England with short wavy leaves in *[Edwards's Botanical Register](/wiki/The_Botanical_Register "The Botanical Register")*, giving it the name *Banksia undulata* "wavy-leaved banksia" but conceding it may have been a variety of *B. serrata*.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Under [Brown's taxonomic arrangement](/wiki/Brown%27s_taxonomic_arrangement_of_Banksia "Brown's taxonomic arrangement of Banksia"), *B. serrata* was placed in [subgenus](/wiki/Subgenus "Subgenus") *[Banksia verae](/wiki/Banksia_verae "Banksia verae")*, the "true banksias", because the inflorescence is a typical *Banksia* flower spike. *Banksia verae* was renamed *[Eubanksia](/wiki/Eubanksia "Eubanksia")* by [Stephan Endlicher](/wiki/Stephan_Ladislaus_Endlicher "Stephan Ladislaus Endlicher") in 1847, and demoted to [sectional](/wiki/Section_(botany) "Section (botany)") rank by [Carl Meissner](/wiki/Carl_Meissner "Carl Meissner") in [his 1856 classification](/wiki/Meissner%27s_taxonomic_arrangement_of_Banksia "Meissner's taxonomic arrangement of Banksia"). Meissner further divided *Eubanksia* into four [series](/wiki/Series_(botany) "Series (botany)"), placing *B. serrata* in series *[Quercinae](/wiki/Banksia_ser._Quercinae "Banksia ser. Quercinae")* on the basis of its toothed leaves.[[15]](#cite_note-Meissner_1856-15) When [George Bentham](/wiki/George_Bentham "George Bentham") published [his 1870 arrangement](/wiki/Bentham%27s_taxonomic_arrangement_of_Banksia "Bentham's taxonomic arrangement of Banksia") in *[Flora Australiensis](/wiki/Flora_Australiensis "Flora Australiensis")*, he discarded Meissner's series, replacing them with four [sections](/wiki/Section_(botany) "Section (botany)"). *B. serrata* was placed in *Orthostylis*, a somewhat heterogeneous section containing 18 species.[[16]](#cite_note-Bentham_1870-16)

In 1891, [Otto Kuntze](/wiki/Otto_Kuntze "Otto Kuntze"), in his *[Revisio Generum Plantarum](/wiki/Revisio_Generum_Plantarum "Revisio Generum Plantarum")*, rejected the generic name *Banksia* [L.f.](/wiki/Carolus_Linnaeus_the_Younger "Carolus Linnaeus the Younger"), on the grounds that the name *Banksia* had previously been published in 1776 as *Banksia* [J.R.Forst](/wiki/Johann_Reinhold_Forster "Johann Reinhold Forster") & [G.Forst](/wiki/Georg_Forster "Georg Forster"), referring to the genus now known as *[Pimelea](/wiki/Pimelea "Pimelea")*. Kuntze proposed *Sirmuellera* as an alternative, referring to this species as *Sirmuellera serrata*.[[17]](#cite_note-17) For the same reason, [James Britten](/wiki/James_Britten "James Britten") transferred the species to the genus *Isostylis* as *Isostylis serrata* in 1905.[[18]](#cite_note-britten_1905-18) These applications of the [principle of priority](/wiki/Principle_of_priority "Principle of priority") were largely ignored,[[19]](#cite_note-19) and *Banksia* L.f. was [formally conserved](/wiki/Conserved_name "Conserved name") and *Sirmuellera* rejected in 1940.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Current placement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Banksia_serrata&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Current placement")]

[Alex George](/wiki/Alex_George_(botanist) "Alex George (botanist)") published a new taxonomic arrangement of *Banksia* in his 1981 monograph "[The genus Banksia L.f. (Proteaceae)](/wiki/The_genus_Banksia_L.f._(Proteaceae) "The genus Banksia L.f. (Proteaceae)")".[[4]](#cite_note-George_1981-4) Endlicher's *Eubanksia* became [*B.* subg. *Banksia*](/wiki/Banksia_subg._Banksia "Banksia subg. Banksia"), and was divided into three sections. *B. serrata* was placed in [*B.* sect. *Banksia*](/wiki/Banksia_sect._Banksia "Banksia sect. Banksia"), and this was further divided into nine series, placing *B. serrata* in [*B.* ser. *Banksia*](/wiki/Banksia_ser._Banksia "Banksia ser. Banksia") (formerly *Orthostylis*).[[21]](#cite_note-George_1999-21)[[a]](#cite_note-22)

In 1996, [Kevin Thiele](/wiki/Kevin_Thiele "Kevin Thiele") and [Pauline Ladiges](/wiki/Pauline_Ladiges "Pauline Ladiges") published a new arrangement for the genus, after [cladistic](/wiki/Cladistics "Cladistics") analyses yielded a [cladogram](/wiki/Cladogram "Cladogram") significantly different from George's arrangement. [Thiele and Ladiges' arrangement](/wiki/Thiele_and_Ladiges%27_taxonomic_arrangement_of_Banksia "Thiele and Ladiges' taxonomic arrangement of Banksia") retained *B. serrata* in series *Banksia*, placing it in [*B.* subser. *Banksia*](/wiki/Banksia_subser._Banksia "Banksia subser. Banksia") along with *B. aemula* as its [sister taxon](/wiki/Sister_taxon "Sister taxon") (united by their unusual seedling leaves) and *B. ornata* as its next closest relative.[[22]](#cite_note-Thiele_1996-23) This arrangement stood until 1999, when George effectively reverted to his 1981 arrangement in his monograph for the *[Flora of Australia](/wiki/Flora_of_Australia_(series) "Flora of Australia (series)")* series.[[21]](#cite_note-George_1999-21)

Under [George's taxonomic arrangement of *Banksia*](/wiki/George%27s_taxonomic_arrangement_of_Banksia "George's taxonomic arrangement of Banksia"), *B. serrata* is placed in the [Genus](/wiki/Genus "Genus") *[Banksia](/wiki/Banksia "Banksia")*, [Subgenus](/wiki/Subgenus "Subgenus") *[Banksia](/wiki/Banksia_subg._Banksia "Banksia subg. Banksia")*, [Section](/wiki/Section_(botany) "Section (botany)") *[Banksia](/wiki/Banksia_sect._Banksia "Banksia sect. Banksia")* and [Series](/wiki/Series_(botany) "Series (botany)") *[Banksia](/wiki/Banksia_ser._Banksia "Banksia ser. Banksia")* along with *B. aemula*, *[B. ornata](/wiki/Banksia_ornata "Banksia ornata")*, *[B. baxteri](/wiki/Banksia_baxteri "Banksia baxteri")*, *[B. speciosa](/wiki/Banksia_speciosa "Banksia speciosa")*, *[B. menziesii](/wiki/Banksia_menziesii "Banksia menziesii")*, *[B. candolleana](/wiki/Banksia_candolleana "Banksia candolleana")* and *[B. sceptrum](/wiki/Banksia_sceptrum "Banksia sceptrum")*.[[21]](#cite_note-George_1999-21)

In 2002, a [molecular](/wiki/Molecular_phylogenetics "Molecular phylogenetics") study by [Austin Mast](/wiki/Austin_Mast "Austin Mast") again showed that the three eastern species formed a natural group, or [clade](/wiki/Clade "Clade"), but they were only distantly related to other members of the series *Banksia*. Instead, they formed a sister group to a large group comprising the series [Prostratae](/wiki/Banksia_ser._Prostratae "Banksia ser. Prostratae"), [Ochraceae](/wiki/Banksia_ser._Ochraceae "Banksia ser. Ochraceae"), [Tetragonae](/wiki/Banksia_ser._Tetragonae "Banksia ser. Tetragonae") (including *Banksia elderiana*), *[Banksia lullfitzii](/wiki/Banksia_lullfitzii "Banksia lullfitzii")* and *[Banksia baueri](/wiki/Banksia_baueri "Banksia baueri")*.[[23]](#cite_note-Mast_2002-24)

In 2005, Mast, Eric Jones and Shawn Havery published the results of their cladistic analyses of [DNA sequence](/wiki/DNA_sequence "DNA sequence") data for *Banksia*. They inferred a [phylogeny](/wiki/Phylogeny "Phylogeny") greatly different from the accepted taxonomic arrangement, including finding *Banksia* to be [paraphyletic](/wiki/Paraphyly "Paraphyly") with respect to *[Dryandra](/wiki/Banksia_ser._Dryandra "Banksia ser. Dryandra")*.[[24]](#cite_note-Mast_2005-25) A new taxonomic arrangement was not published at the time, but early in 2007 Mast and Thiele initiated a rearrangement by transferring *Dryandra* to *Banksia*, and publishing [*B.* subg. *Spathulatae*](/wiki/Banksia_subg._Spathulatae "Banksia subg. Spathulatae") for the species having spoon-shaped [cotyledons](/wiki/Cotyledon "Cotyledon"); in this way they also redefined the [autonym](/wiki/Autonym_(botany) "Autonym (botany)") *B.* subg. *Banksia*. They foreshadowed publishing a full arrangement once DNA [sampling](/wiki/Sampling_(statistics) "Sampling (statistics)") of *Dryandra* was complete. In the meantime, if Mast and Thiele's nomenclatural changes are taken as an interim arrangement, then *B. serrata* is placed in *B.* subg. *Banksia*.[[25]](#cite_note-Mast_2007-26)

### Intraspecific variation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Banksia_serrata&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Intraspecific variation")]

*Banksia serrata* is a fairly uniform species, showing little variation between different habitats other than occasionally occurring as a shrub in coastal areas. No subspecific taxa are recognised.[[4]](#cite_note-George_1981-4) In 1896, [Richard Thomas Baker](/wiki/Richard_Thomas_Baker "Richard Thomas Baker") found a clump of *B. serrata* at Kelgoola on the [Central Tablelands](/wiki/Central_Tablelands "Central Tablelands") with large leaves, with hairs on leaves and stems, further west of any other collection of the species. He named it *B. serrata* var. *hirsuta*.[[26]](#cite_note-27) In his 1981 monograph, George was unable to locate a collection that corresponded with the report.[[4]](#cite_note-George_1981-4)

## Distribution and habitat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Banksia_serrata&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Distribution and habitat")]

*Banksia serrata* occurs on the Australian mainland from [Wilsons Promontory](/wiki/Wilsons_Promontory "Wilsons Promontory"), [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)") (39°08′ S) in the south, to [Maryborough, Queensland](/wiki/Maryborough,_Queensland "Maryborough, Queensland") (25°31′ S) in the north. There is also a large population at [Sisters Creek](/wiki/Sisters_Creek,_Tasmania "Sisters Creek, Tasmania") in [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania") and another in the southwest corner of the [Wingaroo Nature Reserve](/wiki/Wingaroo_Nature_Reserve "Wingaroo Nature Reserve") in the northern part of [Flinders Island](/wiki/Flinders_Island "Flinders Island"). The Wingaroo NR Conservation Plan (2000) reports that the population comprises around 60 to 80 individual trees, the majority of which are believed to be "quite old". It adds that there is evidence of slow and continuous regeneration, which appears to be occurring in the absence of fire.[[27]](#cite_note-book-28)[[28]](#cite_note-tasparks-29)

Throughout its range, *B. serrata* is found on well-drained sandy soils that are low in nutrients, and it is often found on stabilised soil near the coast just behind the main dune system. In the Sydney region it is found with other typical woodland species, including yellow bloodwood (*[Corymbia eximia](/wiki/Corymbia_eximia "Corymbia eximia")*), red bloodwood (*[C. gummifera](/wiki/Corymbia_gummifera "Corymbia gummifera")*), silvertop ash (*[Eucalyptus sieberi](/wiki/Eucalyptus_sieberi "Eucalyptus sieberi")*), blue-leaved stringybark (*[E. agglomerata](/wiki/Eucalyptus_agglomerata "Eucalyptus agglomerata")*) and Sydney peppermint (*[E. piperita](/wiki/Eucalyptus_piperita "Eucalyptus piperita")*).[[29]](#cite_note-Benson-30)

In the Upper [Myall River](/wiki/Myall_River "Myall River") region, *B. serrata* grows in dry sclerophyll forest on sandy soils that have recently formed (in the [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene")) or in shallow soils over differing substrates, while its close relative *B. aemula* grows in dry heath forest that occurs on ancient [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") sands not disturbed in 125,000 years. In intermediate communities both species are found.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

*B. serrata* is a component of the [Eastern Suburbs Banksia Scrub](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_Banksia_Scrub "Eastern Suburbs Banksia Scrub") (ESBS), designated an endangered ecological community. This community is found on windblown sands, which are younger than the heathlands to the north.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Banksia_serrata&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ecology")]

See also: [Ecology of *Banksia*](/wiki/Ecology_of_Banksia "Ecology of Banksia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Calyptorhynchus_funereus_-Rocky_Cape_National_Park-8.jpg/250px-Calyptorhynchus_funereus_-Rocky_Cape_National_Park-8.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calyptorhynchus_funereus_-Rocky_Cape_National_Park-8.jpg)

[Yellow-tailed black cockatoo](/wiki/Yellow-tailed_black_cockatoo "Yellow-tailed black cockatoo") at flowers

This species is a food source for several bird species. Nectar-eating birds that have been observed feeding at the flowers include [bell miner](/wiki/Bell_miner "Bell miner") (*Manorina melanophrys*), [noisy miner](/wiki/Noisy_miner "Noisy miner") (*M. melanocephala*), [white-cheeked honeyeater](/wiki/White-cheeked_honeyeater "White-cheeked honeyeater") (*Phylidonyris nigra*), [New Holland honeyeater](/wiki/New_Holland_honeyeater "New Holland honeyeater") (*P. novaehollandiae*), [crescent honeyeater](/wiki/Crescent_honeyeater "Crescent honeyeater") (*P. pyrrhopterus*), [white-eared honeyeater](/wiki/White-eared_honeyeater "White-eared honeyeater") (*Nesoptilotis leucotis*), [brown honeyeater](/wiki/Brown_honeyeater "Brown honeyeater") (*Lichmera indistincta*), [tawny-crowned honeyeater](/wiki/Tawny-crowned_honeyeater "Tawny-crowned honeyeater") (*Gliciphila melanops*), [white-naped honeyeater](/wiki/White-naped_honeyeater "White-naped honeyeater") (*Melithreptus lunatus*), [white-plumed honeyeater](/wiki/White-plumed_honeyeater "White-plumed honeyeater") (*Ptilotula penicillata*), [yellow-tufted honeyeater](/wiki/Yellow-tufted_honeyeater "Yellow-tufted honeyeater") (*Lichenostomus melanops*), [eastern spinebill](/wiki/Eastern_spinebill "Eastern spinebill") (*Acanthorhynchus tenuirostris*), [red wattlebird](/wiki/Red_wattlebird "Red wattlebird") (*Anthochaera carunculata*), [little wattlebird](/wiki/Little_wattlebird "Little wattlebird") (*A. chrysoptera*), [noisy friarbird](/wiki/Noisy_friarbird "Noisy friarbird") (*Philemon corniculatus*), [spangled drongo](/wiki/Spangled_drongo "Spangled drongo") (*Dicrurus bracteatus*), and [rainbow lorikeet](/wiki/Rainbow_lorikeet "Rainbow lorikeet") (*Trichoglossus moluccanus*).[[5]](#cite_note-Taylor_1988-5)[[29]](#cite_note-Benson-30) The immature follicles are eaten by [yellow-tailed black cockatoo](/wiki/Yellow-tailed_black_cockatoo "Yellow-tailed black cockatoo") (*Zanda funerea*).[[32]](#cite_note-Barker_1984-33)

A 1988 field study found that most flowers of *B. serrata* opened at night, and recorded the [brown antechinus](/wiki/Brown_antechinus "Brown antechinus") (*Antechinus stuartii*), [sugar glider](/wiki/Sugar_glider "Sugar glider") (*Petaurus breviceps*), [eastern pygmy possum](/wiki/Eastern_pygmy_possum "Eastern pygmy possum") (*Cercartetus nanus*), and [bush rat](/wiki/Bush_rat "Bush rat") (*Rattus fuscipes*) as nocturnal mammalian visitors and pollinators.[[33]](#cite_note-34) Other mammals recorded eating the flowers include the [grey-headed flying fox](/wiki/Grey-headed_flying_fox "Grey-headed flying fox") (*Pteropus poliocephalus*), [little red flying fox](/wiki/Little_red_flying_fox "Little red flying fox") (*P. scapulatus*) and [common blossom bat](/wiki/Common_blossom_bat "Common blossom bat") (*Syconycteris australis*).[[29]](#cite_note-Benson-30) *Banksia serrata* is a host plant for the larval and adult stages of the banksia jewel beetle (*[Cyrioides imperialis](/wiki/Cyrioides_imperialis "Cyrioides imperialis")*).[[34]](#cite_note-35) Native bees and [European honey bees](/wiki/Western_honey_bee "Western honey bee") visit the flowers.[[5]](#cite_note-Taylor_1988-5)

*Banksia serrata* has a central [taproot](/wiki/Taproot "Taproot") and few lateral roots. Clusters of fine branched [proteoid roots](/wiki/Cluster_root "Cluster root") up to 15 cm (6 in) long arise from larger roots.[[35]](#cite_note-Purnell-36) These roots are particularly efficient at absorbing nutrients from nutrient-poor soils, such as the [phosphorus](/wiki/Phosphorus "Phosphorus")-deficient native soils of Australia.[[36]](#cite_note-Lamont_1993-37)

*Banksia serrata* has shown a variable susceptibility to dieback from the pathogen *[Phytophthora cinnamomi](/wiki/Phytophthora_cinnamomi "Phytophthora cinnamomi")*, the plants in sandier soils showing more resistance than those in heavier soils. Plants from Wilsons Promontory were sensitive. The resistance of plants from Flinders Island is unknown. The small size of the stand renders it vulnerable to eradication.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

### Response to fire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Banksia_serrata&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Response to fire")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Banksia_serrata_P5270217.jpg/250px-Banksia_serrata_P5270217.jpg)](/wiki/File:Banksia_serrata_P5270217.jpg)

Epicormic regrowth after bushfire

*Banksia serrata* plants generally become fire tolerant by five to seven years of age in that they are able to resprout afterwards.[[29]](#cite_note-Benson-30) Regrowth is generally from [epicormic buds](/wiki/Epicormic_buds "Epicormic buds") under their thick bark if the plant is between 2 and 6 m (7–20 ft) high or possibly from the woody subterranean base known as the [lignotuber](/wiki/Lignotuber "Lignotuber") of younger and smaller plants. There is doubt as to whether recovery from a lignotuber is possible, although it has been demonstrated in other *Banksia* species such as *[B. menziesii](/wiki/Banksia_menziesii "Banksia menziesii")*.[[38]](#cite_note-Mibus_and_Sedgley-39) Stem size is a critical factor; stems with a [DBH](/wiki/Diameter_at_breast_height "Diameter at breast height") of under 1 cm (0.4 in) are unable to withstand low intensity fires. A stem/trunk DBH of 2 cm (0.8 in) is needed to survive low intensity fires and of around 5 cm (2 in) to withstand a high intensity fire.[[39]](#cite_note-bradstock88-40)

As with other species in the genus, *B. serrata* trees are naturally adapted to the presence of regular bushfires and exhibit a form of [serotiny](/wiki/Serotiny "Serotiny") known as [pyriscence](/wiki/Pyriscence "Pyriscence"). The seedbank in the plant's canopy is released after bushfire. Fire intervals of 10 to 15 years are recommended for the Eastern Suburbs Banksia Scrub, as longer leads to overgrowth by coastal tea tree (*[Leptospermum laevigatum](/wiki/Leptospermum_laevigatum "Leptospermum laevigatum")*),[[40]](#cite_note-41) while an interval of at least nine years was indicated in a study at [Brisbane Water National Park](/wiki/Brisbane_Water_National_Park "Brisbane Water National Park").[[41]](#cite_note-bradstock90-42) Repeated intervals of five years' duration or less will result in decline of population as young plants are not yet resistant to fire, and their tall [habit](/wiki/Habit_(biology) "Habit (biology)") makes them especially vulnerable.[[39]](#cite_note-bradstock88-40) The seedbank is most productive between 25 and 35 years after a previous fire, although seedlings may be outcompeted by seedlings of [obligate](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/obligate#Adjective "wikt:obligate") seeder species.[[41]](#cite_note-bradstock90-42) A field study found that seeds were dispersed up to 4 m (13 ft) from the parent plant in an hour by strong wind.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Seeds are also released spontaneously in the absence of fire. The degree to which *B. serrata* trees exhibit [bradyspory](/wiki/Bradyspory "Bradyspory") seems to depend on the nature of the site where they grow. One study recorded plants at coastal sites having more than 30% of their follicles open, compared to those further inland having fewer than 5% open. Follicles also open when part of the tree dies.[[43]](#cite_note-Whelan-44)

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Banksia_serrata&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Uses")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Banksia_serrata_%27Pygmy_Possum%27.jpg/250px-Banksia_serrata_%27Pygmy_Possum%27.jpg)](/wiki/File:Banksia_serrata_%27Pygmy_Possum%27.jpg)

[*Banksia* 'Pygmy Possum'](/wiki/Banksia_%27Pygmy_Possum%27 "Banksia 'Pygmy Possum'") at the [Royal Botanic Gardens, Cranbourne](/wiki/Royal_Botanic_Gardens,_Cranbourne "Royal Botanic Gardens, Cranbourne")

### Use in horticulture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Banksia_serrata&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Use in horticulture")]

The gnarled lumpy bark, saw-toothed leaves and silvery-yellow spikes in bud are horticultural features of *B. serrata*.[[44]](#cite_note-Collins-45) It can be grown readily from seed, collected after heating the "cone". A sterile, free-draining seed-raising mixture prevents [damping off](/wiki/Damping_off "Damping off").[[45]](#cite_note-seed-46) In cultivation, though relatively resistant to *P. cinnamomi* dieback, it grows best in a well-drained soil, preferably fairly sandy with a [pH](/wiki/PH "PH") from 5.5 to 7.5, and a sunny aspect.[[44]](#cite_note-Collins-45) Summer watering aids in growth. The plant may take several years to flower, although plants grown from cuttings may flower in two years.[[44]](#cite_note-Collins-45) *Banksia serrata* is also used in [bonsai](/wiki/Bonsai "Bonsai").[[46]](#cite_note-47)

### Use in construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Banksia_serrata&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Use in construction")]

Red-pink in colour, the timber resembles English oak.[[44]](#cite_note-Collins-45) It has been used in boatbuilding and is strong, durable and distinctively patterned.[[8]](#cite_note-Wrigley_1991-8)

### Cultivars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Banksia_serrata&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Cultivars")]

* [*Banksia* 'Pygmy Possum'](/wiki/Banksia_%27Pygmy_Possum%27 "Banksia 'Pygmy Possum'") – first propagated by Austraflora Nursery, this is a prostrate form originally from the [Green Cape](/wiki/Green_Cape,_New_South_Wales "Green Cape, New South Wales") area on the New South Wales far south coast. Similar plants are seen in nurseries called simply *B. serrata* (Prostrate) collected from the same area. This plant is suitable for rockeries and small gardens.[[47]](#cite_note-48)
* [*Banksia* 'Superman'](/wiki/Banksia_%27Superman%27 "Banksia 'Superman'") – selection from large-flowered (spikes to 27 cm (11 in) high) and large-leaved population from [Scotts Head](/wiki/Scotts_Head,_New_South_Wales "Scotts Head, New South Wales") on New South Wales mid north coast. As yet, it is not in commercial cultivation, though is registered with [ACRA](/wiki/Australian_Cultivar_Registration_Authority "Australian Cultivar Registration Authority").[[48]](#cite_note-49)
